91.title: Kad bih… — the hypothetical engine
91.intro: Today the conditional stops being a table and becomes conversation. The pattern: kad + conditional in the condition, conditional again in the result — and you chain results with 'a onda bih…' (and then I would…). Say every line aloud; the goal is that 'bih' comes out before you think about it.

items.hr: Kad bih imao vremena, učio bih hrvatski svaki dan.
items.en: If I had time, I would study Croatian every day.
items.note: Both halves take the conditional: kad bih imao…, učio bih.

items.hr: Kad bih dobio slobodnu godinu, otputovao bih u Dalmaciju.
items.en: If I got a year off, I would travel to Dalmatia.
items.note: dobio → otputovao: perfective participles for one-off events.

items.en: …and then I would learn to surf.
items.note: Chain hypotheticals with a onda bih… — this is how you sustain a monologue.

items.hr: Kad bih dobio dobre vijesti, prvo bih javio ženi.
items.en: If I got good news, I would tell my wife first.
items.note: javiti komu — dative! One of your dative-rection verbs.

items.hr: Kome bi prvo javio dobre vijesti?
items.en: Who would you tell the good news first?
items.note: kome = dative of tko. The answer needs a dative too.

items.hr: Što bi radio kad bi imao milijun eura?
items.en: What would you do if you had a million euros?
items.note: bi for ti — the full paradigm: bih, bi, bi, bismo, biste, bi.

items.hr: Kupio bih stan blizu mora i pomogao bih roditeljima.
items.en: I would buy a flat near the sea and help my parents.
items.note: pomoći komu — dative again: pomogao bih roditeljima.

items.hr: Kad bismo imali više vremena, putovali bismo češće.
items.en: If we had more time, we would travel more often.
items.note: bismo — the careful standard form for 'we'; colloquial speech flattens everything to bi.

items.hr: Pisao bih svaki dan. / Napisao bih knjigu.
items.en: I would write every day. / I would write a book (and finish it).
items.note: Aspect lives on in the conditional: imperfective for habit, perfective for one completed act.

items.en: I wouldn't change anything.
items.note: ne + bih; ništa stays with the negated verb — Croatian requires the double negative.

items.hr: Bi li i ti otišao sa mnom?
items.en: Would you leave with me too?
items.note: Question: bi + li, then the participle. (sa mnom = with me.)

items.hr: Javio bih ti se čim bih stigao.
items.en: I would get in touch with you as soon as I arrived.
items.note: Clitic chain in action: bih (aux) → ti (dative) → se.

91.title: Hypothetical workout
91.intro: Auxiliaries, datives, aspect and clitic order — everything a free hypothetical needs, drilled in isolation and then combined.

questions.prompt: Kad ___ imao vremena, putovao bih. (ja)
questions.explanation: First person singular takes bih: kad bih imao. Colloquial speech says bi, but the standard — and this course — keeps bih.

questions.prompt: The conditional auxiliary for mi (we) is:
questions.explanation: mi bismo, vi biste — the two forms that mark careful speech. bihmo doesn't exist; biste is for vi.

questions.prompt: Kome bi prvo javio dobre vijesti? — ___
options: Javio bih ih od svoje žene.
questions.explanation: javiti komu takes the dative: svojoj ženi. And it's svoj because the subject (I) is the possessor.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Kad bih imao milijun eura, ___ bih stan.' (buy — one completed purchase)
questions.explanation: One completed act → perfective kupiti → participle kupio. Kupovao bih would mean 'I would keep buying'.

questions.prompt: Complete with the right auxiliary: 'Mi ___ putovali češće.'
questions.explanation: mi → bismo: Mi bismo putovali češće. This is exactly the form colloquial speech loses — using it correctly is an instant level-up.

questions.prompt: 'I would write to you every day' — which aspect?
questions.explanation: svaki dan = a repeated habit → imperfective pisati → pisao bih. Napisao bih is for one finished piece of writing.

questions.prompt: Build the chained hypothetical: 'If I got a year off, I would travel to Dalmatia.'
questions.answer: Kad bih dobio slobodnu godinu, otputovao bih u Dalmaciju.
questions.explanation: Both clauses carry the conditional: Kad bih dobio…, otputovao bih… In the second clause bih sits in second position, right after the participle.

questions.prompt: Which clitic order is correct for 'I would get in touch with you tomorrow'?
questions.explanation: The chain: auxiliary (bih) → dative (ti) → se. Javio bih ti se — the same template as with sam/ću: aux before dative, se last.

91.title: Što bi bilo kad bi… — dream out loud
91.intro: A whole conversation held together by the conditional. Read your lines aloud, then swap roles — you'll need her feminine participles (radila, dobila) when you ask the questions.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Igrajmo igru: što bi radila kad bi dobila slobodnu godinu?
lines.en: Let's play a game: what would you do if you got a year off?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Hm, kad bih dobila slobodnu godinu, otputovala bih na Havaje. A ti?
lines.en: Hm, if I got a year off, I would travel to Hawaii. And you?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja bih kupio brod i plovio bih po Jadranu.
lines.en: I would buy a boat and sail around the Adriatic.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ti ne znaš ploviti!
lines.en: You don't know how to sail!

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Zato bih prvo naučio! A onda bih te odveo na Hvar.
lines.en: That's why I would learn first! And then I would take you to Hvar.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Dobro. A kome bi prvo javio da si dobio slobodnu godinu?
lines.en: Fine. And who would you tell first that you got a year off?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Tebi, naravno. Odmah bih ti javio.
lines.en: You, of course. I would tell you right away.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Točno! 'Javio bih ti' — dativ. Bravo.
lines.en: Correct! 'Javio bih ti' — dative. Bravo.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: A kad bismo imali milijun eura, kupili bismo kuću uz more.
lines.en: And if we had a million euros, we would buy a house by the sea.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Kad bismo… kupili bismo — super, nisi rekao 'bi'!
lines.en: Kad bismo… kupili bismo — great, you didn't say 'bi'!

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Polako postajem pravi Hrvat.
lines.en: I'm slowly becoming a real Croat.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ne bih išla tako daleko, ali govoriš sve bolje!
lines.en: I wouldn't go that far, but you're speaking better and better!

92.title: Model text: Moji planovi i želje
92.intro: Before you write your 120 words, dissect a model. Each sentence shows one tool: futur I for real plans, the conditional for wishes, the dative for the people involved, and svoj wherever the subject owns. Read it aloud as one connected text, then write yours by swapping in your own life.

items.en: This year I have big plans.
items.note: A plain present opener — no need to start fancy.

items.hr: U rujnu ću položiti ispit iz hrvatskoga.
items.en: In September I will pass my Croatian exam.
items.note: Real plan → futur I. položiti = perfective: pass it, not just sit it.

items.hr: Tražit ću novi posao u Zagrebu.
items.en: I will look for a new job in Zagreb.
items.note: Infinitive-first futur: tražiti drops the final -i in writing → Tražit ću.

items.hr: Svaki dan ću učiti trideset minuta.
items.en: I will study thirty minutes every day.
items.note: Something else opens the sentence → the clitic ću slots into second position.

items.en: I would like to speak Croatian fluently.
items.note: Wish → conditional. htio for a man, htjela for a woman.

items.hr: Najviše bih volio raditi od kuće.
items.en: Most of all I would like to work from home.
items.note: najviše bih volio — the superlative wish, a lovely A2 flex.

items.hr: Želim pomoći svojim roditeljima oko kuće.
items.en: I want to help my (own) parents around the house.
items.note: pomoći komu → dative plural: svojim roditeljima. Subject = owner → svoj.

items.hr: Ženi ću kupiti poklon za godišnjicu.
items.en: I will buy my wife a present for our anniversary.
items.note: Recipient in the dative (ženi), thing in the accusative (poklon).

items.hr: Kad bih imao više slobodnog vremena, češće bih zvao brata.
items.en: If I had more free time, I would call my brother more often.
items.note: zvao (imperfective) — regular calls, a habit.

items.hr: Vjerujem da će sve moje želje postati planovi.
items.en: I believe that all my wishes will become plans.
items.note: A neat closing line — steal it for your own text.

92.title: Self-check: the traps in your text
92.intro: Before you read your text to your partner, hunt these errors yourself. Each item shows the classic wrong version and the fix — exactly the checklist a Croatian teacher runs through learner writing.

items.hr: ✗ Ja bi došao → ✓ Ja bih došao
items.en: I would come
items.note: First person needs bih. 'Ja bi' is the single most common conditional slip.

items.hr: ✗ Mi bi putovali → ✓ Mi bismo putovali
items.en: we would travel
items.note: bismo for mi, biste for vi — the exam favourites.

items.hr: ✗ Pomažem moje roditelje → ✓ Pomažem svojim roditeljima
items.en: I help my parents
items.note: Two fixes at once: pomagati takes the dative, and subject = owner → svoj.

items.hr: ✗ Bih htio kavu → ✓ Htio bih kavu
items.en: I would like a coffee
items.note: bih is a clitic — it can never stand first in the sentence.

items.hr: ✗ Sutra ću da radim → ✓ Sutra ću raditi
items.en: Tomorrow I will work
items.note: Croatian futur I takes the infinitive; 'da + present' here is Serbian, not Croatian.

items.hr: ✗ Kupit ću poklon mojoj ženi → ✓ Kupit ću poklon svojoj ženi
items.en: I'll buy my (own) wife a present
items.note: The subject owns → svoj beats moj every time.

items.hr: ✗ Javit ću se ti sutra → ✓ Javit ću ti se sutra
items.en: I'll get in touch with you tomorrow
items.note: Clitic order: ću (aux) → ti (dative) → se.

items.hr: ✗ Kad bih ću imati vremena… → ✓ Kad budem imao vremena… / Kad bih imao vremena…
items.en: when / if I have time
items.note: Pick one mood: futur II for a real future plan, conditional for a daydream — never both at once.

92.title: Edit your own text
92.intro: Editor mode: every question here is an error you might have just written. Catch them now and your partner will have nothing left to correct.

questions.prompt: A male speaker says 'I would come'. Which is correct?
questions.explanation: ja → bih, and a male speaker uses the masculine participle došao. došla would be a female speaker.

questions.prompt: A real plan for tomorrow — which sentence is correct?
questions.explanation: Plan → futur I: ću + infinitive. With Sutra first, ću must come second — 'Sutra radit ću' breaks the clitic rule, and 'ću da radim' isn't Croatian.

questions.prompt: Pomažem ___ .
questions.explanation: pomagati governs the dative: roditeljima. And because the subject owns them, svoj is the right possessive — svojim roditeljima.

questions.prompt: Complete the wish: 'Htio ___ tečno govoriti hrvatski.'
questions.explanation: Wish → conditional: Htio bih tečno govoriti hrvatski. The participle opens the sentence, bih follows in second position.

questions.prompt: Turn the fact into a wish: 'Volim raditi od kuće.' → 'Najviše ___ ___ raditi od kuće.' (male speaker)
questions.explanation: Najviše bih volio raditi od kuće. — najviše opens, so bih takes second position, then the participle volio.

questions.prompt: Fix the clitic order and write the whole corrected sentence: 'Javit ću se ti sutra.'
questions.explanation: Dative before se: Javit ću ti se sutra. The chain is aux (ću) → dative (ti) → se.

questions.prompt: Build the sentence: 'If I had more free time, I would call my brother more often.'
questions.answer: Kad bih imao više slobodnog vremena, češće bih zvao brata.
questions.explanation: više slobodnog vremena — quantity + genitive. In the result clause, češće opens and bih again takes second position.

questions.prompt: Kupit ću ___ poklon. (for my wife — the recipient)
questions.explanation: The recipient goes in the dative: svojoj ženi. svoju ženu would be the accusative — that would make her the thing being bought!

93.title: Na kolodvoru — 14 transport words
93.intro: The station vocabulary that runs every trip in Croatia. Learn these fourteen and you can read any departures board and survive any ticket window. Watch two old friends inside the list: the fleeting a (polazak → polaska) and sibilarization (putnik → putnici).

items.en: (train or bus) station
items.note: glavni kolodvor = the main train station; autobusni kolodvor = bus station.

items.en: platform
items.note: S kojeg perona…? = from which platform (s + genitive).

items.en: departure
items.note: Fleeting a: polazak → polaska (G sg.). Polasci = the departures board.

items.en: arrival
items.note: Same family as dolaziti (to come/arrive).

items.en: transfer, changing (trains/buses)
items.note: From presjedati; the perfective verb is presjesti: Morate presjesti u Ogulinu.

items.en: timetable
items.note: Literally 'order of driving' — vožnje is a genitive.

items.en: delay
items.note: Vlak ima kašnjenje = vlak kasni — both mean the train is late.

items.en: one-way ticket
items.note: smjer = direction; u jednom smjeru = in one direction (locative).

items.en: return ticket
items.note: From povratak (return) — the cheaper way to travel twice.

items.en: train, bus, tram
items.note: Your three everyday vehicles — all masculine.

items.en: stop (bus, tram)
items.note: autobusna stanica; tramvajsko stajalište — you'll hear both.

items.en: driver
items.note: Soft č → instrumental vozačem, not 'vozačom'.

items.en: passenger, traveller
items.note: Plural putnici — k → c before -i, sibilarization strikes again.

items.en: luggage
items.note: Always singular in Croatian, like 'luggage' in English.

93.title: Tramvajem, autom, pješice — how do you get around?
93.intro: Means of transport go in the instrumental with NO preposition: tramvajem, vlakom, autom. (English says 'by tram'; Croatian just declines.) The one exception: pješice (on foot) is an adverb — no case at all. Then come the three timetable questions you'll actually ask.

items.en: I go to work by tram.
items.note: tramvaj ends in soft j → instrumental -em: tramvajem.

items.en: I travel by train.
items.note: Hard consonant → -om: vlakom.

items.en: Are you going by car or by bus?
items.note: Foreign noun auto declines like a masculine: auta, autu, autom.

items.en: I ride a bicycle.
items.note: voziti se = to ride — don't forget the se.

items.en: I'm flying to London by plane.
I go on foot.
items.note: pješice (also pješke) is an adverb — the one 'means of transport' with no instrumental.

items.en: When does the train to Rijeka leave?
items.note: Destination: za + accusative — vlak za Rijeku.

items.hr: S kojeg perona polazi vlak za Split?
items.en: From which platform does the train to Split leave?
items.note: s + genitive: s kojeg perona, s perona broj dva.

items.hr: Koliko košta povratna karta za Zadar?
items.en: How much is a return ticket to Zadar?

items.hr: Vlak polazi u osam i deset s perona broj dva.
items.en: The train leaves at 8:10 from platform two.
items.note: Your answer template: time (u + A), then platform (s + G).

items.hr: Moram li presjedati? — Ne, vlak je izravan.
items.en: Do I have to change trains? — No, the train is direct.
items.note: izravan = direct; also izravni vlak / izravna linija.

93.title: Transport workout
93.intro: Words first, then the instrumental of means, then full timetable questions — exactly the ladder from flashcard to ticket window.

questions.prompt: 'Platform' in Croatian is:
questions.explanation: peron = platform. polazak = departure, presjedanje = transfer, prtljaga = luggage.

questions.prompt: Vlak ___ dvadeset minuta.
questions.explanation: kasniti = to be late: Vlak kasni dvadeset minuta. kašnjenje is the noun (delay), kasno the adverb (late).

questions.prompt: Idem na posao ___ . (by tram)
questions.explanation: Means of transport → bare instrumental: tramvajem. No preposition — 'u tramvaj' would mean 'into the tram'.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Putujem ___ .' (by train — vlak)
questions.explanation: vlak + -om → vlakom. Hard final consonant takes -om; soft ones (tramvaj, vozač) take -em.

questions.prompt: The plural of putnik (passenger) is:
questions.explanation: Sibilarization: k → c before -i, so putnik → putnici. (putnike is the accusative plural.)

questions.prompt: Ask from which platform the train to Split leaves: 'S ___ perona polazi vlak za Split?'
questions.explanation: s + genitive: s kojeg(a) perona — 'from which platform'. koji declines like an adjective: kojeg, kojem, kojim.

questions.prompt: Translate: 'I go on foot.'
questions.explanation: Idem pješice. — pješice is an adverb, so no case ending at all. pješke is an equally correct variant.

questions.prompt: Build the announcement: 'The train to Rijeka leaves at nine from platform three.'
questions.answer: Vlak za Rijeku polazi u devet s perona broj tri.
questions.explanation: The full template: vlak za + A (destination), u + time, s perona broj + number. Memorize it whole — you'll hear it verbatim on day 96.

94.title: The pišem class: -ati verbs that change their skin
94.intro: A whole family of -ati verbs refuses the -am pattern: their present softens the final stem consonant (s→š, z→ž, k→č, g→ž, h→š) and takes -em endings. Once you know the ja-form, the whole paradigm follows. These are not rare verbs — pisati, kazati and slati are some of the most frequent words in the language.

items.hr: pisati → pišem, pišeš, piše, pišemo, pišete, pišu
items.en: to write → I write…
items.note: s → š. The model verb of the class.

items.hr: kazati → kažem, kažeš, kaže, kažemo, kažete, kažu
items.en: to say → I say…
items.note: z → ž. Kako se kaže…? = How do you say…?

items.hr: slati → šaljem, šalješ, šalje, šaljemo, šaljete, šalju
items.en: to send → I send…
items.note: The wildest change in the class: sl- → šalj-. Just memorize šaljem.

items.en: to cry → I cry…
items.note: k → č.

items.en: to jump → I jump
items.note: k → č again.

items.en: to shout → I shout
items.note: k → č — the third of the k-verbs.

items.en: to lie (tell untruths) → I lie
items.note: g → ž.

items.en: to wave → I wave
items.note: h → š.

items.en: to breathe → I breathe
items.note: s → š, like pisati.

items.en: to show → I show
items.note: A prefix doesn't change the class: pokazati works exactly like kazati.

items.en: to watch → I watch
items.note: Most -ati verbs are still regular -am. This class must be learned verb by verb — the dictionary always gives you the ja-form.

items.hr: Past is tame: pisati → pisao/pisala; slati → slao/slala
items.en: wrote; sent
items.note: The participle comes from the infinitive stem, so no consonant change in the perfekt.

94.title: Writing and sending — the verbs in action
94.intro: The class in real sentences about messages, letters and noise. Notice how often these verbs pair with a dative person: pišem TI, šaljem TI, mašem TI.

items.en: I'm writing you a message.
items.note: Dative ti + accusative poruku — the double-object pattern.

items.en: What do you say to that?
items.note: A high-frequency idiom: kazati na + accusative.

items.en: I'm sending you photos from the seaside.

items.hr: Ona uvijek kaže istinu, a on često laže.
items.en: She always tells the truth, and he often lies.
items.note: kaže vs laže — both class members, side by side.

items.hr: Djeca skaču i viču u parku.
items.en: The children are jumping and shouting in the park.
items.note: 3rd plural: skaču, viču — the -u ending of the -em class.

items.en: The baby cries all night.
I'm waving to you from the window!
items.note: mahati komu — dative again.

items.en: I breathe deeply when I'm nervous.
Show me what you're writing!
items.note: Imperative from the present stem: pokažem → pokaži!

items.en: Every week we send letters to grandma.
items.note: baki = dative of baka (recipient).

94.title: pišem–kažem–šaljem drill
94.intro: Conjugate the class cold, spot the impostor, then produce full sentences about writing and sending.

questions.prompt: pisati — the ja-form is:
questions.explanation: s → š + -em ending: pišem. 'pisam' is the classic wrong guess from the -am class.

questions.prompt: kazati — on/ona ___:
questions.explanation: z → ž: kažem, kažeš, kaže. kažem is the ja-form, not the on-form.

questions.prompt: slati — the ja-form is:
questions.explanation: slati → šaljem — the biggest stem change in the class. All six forms keep šalj-: šalješ, šalje, šaljemo…

questions.prompt: Give the oni-form of pisati: 'Oni ___ pisma.'
questions.explanation: The -em class ends 3rd plural in -u: pišu, kažu, šalju, plaču.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Beba ___ cijelu noć.' (plakati)
questions.explanation: k → č: plakati → plačem, plačeš, plače. The baby cries all night.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Zašto ___? Reci istinu!' (lagati, ti-form)
questions.explanation: g → ž: lagati → lažem, lažeš. Why are you lying? Tell the truth!

questions.prompt: Which verb does NOT belong to the pišem class?
questions.explanation: gledati is a regular -am verb: gledam. The others soften their consonant: pišem, kažem, mašem.

questions.prompt: Build the sentence: 'Every week we send letters to grandma.'
questions.explanation: šaljemo (mi-form of slati) + accusative pisma + dative recipient baki.

questions.prompt: Translate: 'I'm sending you a message.'
questions.explanation: Šaljem ti poruku. — šaljem (not 'slam'), dative ti, accusative poruku.

95.title: Week 13 in ten lines
95.intro: Everything this week taught, one line each. Say each Croatian line aloud before reading the note — any hesitation marks your weakest day, and that's the exercise set to redo before the conversation below.

items.hr: Kad bih imao vremena, učio bih više.
items.en: If I had time, I would study more.
items.note: Day 91: conditional in both halves of the hypothetical.

items.hr: Kome bi prvo javio? — Svojoj ženi.
items.en: Who would you tell first? — My (own) wife.
items.note: Day 91: javiti + dative; svoj because the subject owns.

items.hr: Mi bismo putovali, vi biste ostali.
items.en: We would travel, you would stay.
items.note: Days 91-92: bismo/biste — the careful forms.

items.en: I would like to speak Croatian fluently.
items.note: Day 92: wishes in the conditional.

items.en: Tomorrow I will look for a new job.
items.note: Day 92: real plans in futur I, ću in second position.

items.hr: Idem na posao tramvajem, a vraćam se pješice.
items.en: I go to work by tram and come back on foot.
items.note: Day 93: instrumental of means; pješice is an adverb.

items.hr: S kojeg perona polazi vlak za Rijeku?
items.en: From which platform does the train to Rijeka leave?
items.note: Day 93: the timetable question — s + genitive, za + accusative.

items.hr: povratna karta, red vožnje, kašnjenje, presjedanje
items.en: return ticket, timetable, delay, transfer
items.note: Day 93: the four station words you can't travel without.

items.hr: pisati → pišem, slati → šaljem, kazati → kažem
items.en: write, send, say
items.note: Day 94: the consonant-softening -em class.

items.en: Show me what you're writing!
items.note: Day 94: the imperative grows out of the present stem: pokažem → pokaži.

95.title: Week 13 mixed review
95.intro: All four topics shuffled, the way real speech serves them. No labels — you decide whether each question needs a conditional, an instrumental, a dative or the pišem class.

questions.prompt: How do you say 'by bus'?
questions.explanation: Means of transport → bare instrumental: autobusom.

questions.prompt: slati — the mi-form is:
questions.explanation: slati → šalj- in every present form: šaljem, šalješ, šalje, šaljemo…

questions.prompt: Ja ___ došao, ali nemam vremena.
questions.explanation: ja → bih: Ja bih došao, ali nemam vremena. (I would come, but I have no time.)

questions.prompt: Koliko košta povratna karta ___ Zadar?
questions.explanation: Tickets and trains take za + accusative for the destination: karta za Zadar, vlak za Rijeku.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Kad ___ imali novca, kupili bismo brod.' (mi)
questions.explanation: mi → bismo in both clauses: Kad bismo imali…, kupili bismo…

questions.prompt: Pomogao bih ___ . (my own brother)
questions.explanation: pomoći komu → dative: svom(u) bratu. svog brata is the accusative/genitive — wrong case for pomoći.

questions.prompt: Translate: 'I would send you a photo.' (poslati, fotografija — male speaker)
questions.explanation: Poslao bih ti fotografiju. — participle first, then the chain bih (aux) → ti (dative), and fotografiju in the accusative.

questions.prompt: Build the sentence: 'If I got a job in Zagreb, I would travel by train every day.'
questions.answer: Kad bih dobio posao u Zagrebu, putovao bih vlakom svaki dan.
questions.explanation: The whole week in one sentence: conditional in both clauses, u Zagrebu (locative), vlakom (instrumental of means).

95.title: Kako se voziš — i što bi promijenio?
95.intro: The week 13 spoken checkpoint: your commute in the instrumental, then city fixes in the conditional. Read it aloud, then improvise your real answers to her questions.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Hajde, mali test prije večere: kako ideš na posao?
lines.en: Come on, a little test before dinner: how do you get to work?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Idem tramvajem do kolodvora, a onda pješice deset minuta.
lines.en: I go by tram to the station, and then ten minutes on foot.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Odlično — tramvajem, instrumental! A što bi promijenio u našem gradu?
lines.en: Excellent — tramvajem, instrumental! And what would you change in our city?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Kad bih ja bio gradonačelnik, tramvaji nikad ne bi kasnili.
lines.en: If I were the mayor, the trams would never be late.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ha! A što biste još napravili, gospodine gradonačelniče?
lines.en: Ha! And what else would you do, Mr. Mayor?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Kupio bih sto novih autobusa i izgradio bih više biciklističkih staza.
lines.en: I would buy a hundred new buses and build more bike paths.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Izgradio bih — bravo! A bi li se ti vozio na posao biciklom?
lines.en: Izgradio bih — bravo! And would YOU ride a bike to work?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ne bih. Ja bih se vozio službenim autom, naravno.
lines.en: I wouldn't. I would ride in an official car, of course.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Tipičan političar! Sve bi promijenio, ali autom bi se vozio.
lines.en: A typical politician! You'd change everything, but you'd still drive.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Šalim se. Najviše volim ići pješice — s tobom.
lines.en: I'm joking. What I like most is going on foot — with you.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Dobar spas. Za tu rečenicu dobivaš kavu.
lines.en: Nice save. For that sentence you get a coffee.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: A kad bih dobio i kolač, bio bih najsretniji čovjek u gradu.
lines.en: And if I also got a cake, I would be the happiest man in town.

96.title: Announcement decoder
96.intro: Station announcements recycle the same dozen phrases. Learn the skeleton and you'll always catch the four essentials: destination, time, platform, delay. Read each line aloud in your best loudspeaker voice — today's exercise plays them to you as audio.

items.hr: Vlak za Split polazi u devet i trideset s perona broj tri.
items.en: The train to Split departs at 9:30 from platform three.
items.note: The master template: destination (za + A), time (u…), platform (s perona broj…).

items.hr: Vlak iz Osijeka stiže na peron broj jedan.
items.en: The train from Osijek is arriving at platform one.
items.note: Arrivals: iz + G (where from), na + A (which platform).

items.hr: Vlak za Rijeku kasni petnaest minuta.
items.en: The train to Rijeka is fifteen minutes late.
items.note: kasni + the time in the accusative. The word you dread.

items.en: because of works on the track
items.note: zbog + genitive — the all-purpose railway excuse. pruga → na pruzi (g → z, sibilarization in the locative!).

items.en: because of traffic congestion
items.note: The bus-station version of the same excuse.

items.hr: Molimo putnike da ne ostavljaju prtljagu bez nadzora.
items.en: We ask passengers not to leave luggage unattended.
items.note: Molimo + accusative + da… — the security announcement on loop at every station.

items.hr: Autobus za Zadar polazi sa stajališta broj pet.
items.en: The bus to Zadar departs from stop number five.
items.note: s becomes sa before st-: sa stajališta.

items.en: Next stop: Ban Jelačić Square.
items.note: The tram announcement you'll hear daily in Zagreb.

items.hr: Putnici za Split presjedaju u Ogulinu.
items.en: Passengers for Split change trains in Ogulin.
items.note: presjedati + u + locative of the transfer town.

items.hr: Vlak je upravo stigao. / Vlak je otkazan.
items.en: The train has just arrived. / The train is cancelled.
items.note: otkazan — the only word worse than kašnjenje.

items.hr: u devet i deset — u pola deset — u petnaest do deset
items.note: pola deset = HALF OF THE TENTH HOUR = 9:30, not 10:30. The number-one listening trap.

96.title: At the ticket window — with a complication
96.intro: Your complete ticket-buying script, plot twists included: a transfer AND a delay. Read both roles aloud twice — the second time, cover the English. This is the role-play; perform it with your partner tonight.

items.hr: Dobar dan, jednu povratnu kartu za Rijeku, molim.
items.en: Good afternoon, one return ticket to Rijeka, please.
items.note: jednu povratnu kartu — the whole phrase in the accusative.

items.hr: Za kada? — Za danas, sljedeći vlak.
items.en: For when? — For today, the next train.

items.hr: Prvi ili drugi razred? — Drugi razred, molim.
items.en: First or second class? — Second class, please.
That's eighteen euros.
items.note: After 5+ the noun goes genitive plural: osamnaest eura.

items.hr: Kada polazi vlak? — U deset i dvadeset s perona broj dva.
items.en: When does the train leave? — At 10:20 from platform two.

items.hr: Moram li presjedati? — Da, presjedate u Ogulinu.
items.en: Do I have to change? — Yes, you change in Ogulin.
items.note: Complication no. 1: the transfer. presjedate — polite vi-form.

items.hr: Koliko traje putovanje? — Oko četiri sata.
items.en: How long does the trip take? — About four hours.
items.note: trajati = to last; četiri sata — the paucal form after 2-4.

items.en: Unfortunately the train is twenty minutes late.
items.note: Complication no. 2: the delay. nažalost = unfortunately.

items.hr: Mogu li platiti karticom? — Naravno. Izvolite kartu.
items.en: Can I pay by card? — Of course. Here's your ticket.
items.note: platiti karticom — the instrumental of means strikes again.

items.en: Thank you. Goodbye! — Have a good trip!
items.note: Sretan put — what they'll say as they hand you the ticket.

96.title: Listening: catch the essentials
96.intro: Three real announcements, played as audio only. From each, extract the essentials — destination, time, platform, delay — then finish with the phrases and the pola-deset trap on paper.

questions.prompt: Listen to the announcement: where is the train going, and when?
questions.explanation: Vlak za Split polazi u devet i trideset s perona broj tri. — za Split (destination), u devet i trideset (9:30).
questions.audioText: Vlak za Split polazi u devet i trideset s perona broj tri.

questions.prompt: Listen: which platform, and how late is the train?
questions.explanation: Vlak za Rijeku polazi s perona broj četiri. Zbog gužve u prometu vlak kasni deset minuta. — platform four, ten minutes' delay.
questions.audioText: Vlak za Rijeku polazi s perona broj četiri. Zbog gužve u prometu vlak kasni deset minuta.

questions.prompt: 'zbog radova na pruzi' means:
options: because of works on the track
questions.answer: because of works on the track
questions.explanation: zbog + G (because of) + radovi (works) + na pruzi (on the track). The standard railway excuse.

questions.prompt: Listen and type the delay as one word (the number you hear).
questions.explanation: Zbog radova na pruzi vlak za Osijek kasni dvadeset minuta. — twenty minutes late.
questions.audioText: Zbog radova na pruzi vlak za Osijek kasni dvadeset minuta.

questions.prompt: 'Putnici za Split presjedaju u Ogulinu.' — what must Split passengers do?
options: Buy a new ticket in Ogulin
questions.explanation: presjedati = to change trains; u Ogulinu = in Ogulin (locative). Your complication word from the ticket window.

questions.prompt: Ask the clerk whether you have to change trains.
questions.explanation: Moram li presjedati? — modal + li in second position. Moram li presjesti? (perfective) is just as good.

questions.prompt: Rebuild the announcement: 'The train to Zadar departs at eight from platform one.'
questions.answer: Vlak za Zadar polazi u osam s perona broj jedan.
questions.explanation: Destination → time → platform: the fixed skeleton of every departure announcement.

questions.prompt: The announcement says the bus leaves 'u pola deset'. That is:
questions.explanation: pola deset = half of the TENTH hour = 9:30. Croatian counts the hour being filled, not the hour just passed — the classic trap that costs learners their bus.

97.title: Aspect pairs for staying in touch
97.intro: Communication verbs come in pairs: the imperfective for the process or habit, the perfective for one completed contact. English hides this ('he called' vs 'he was calling'); Croatian builds it into the verb. Learn each pair as one unit — imperfective / perfective.

items.en: to be calling / to call (once)
items.note: Zvao me cijeli dan. (kept calling) vs Nazvao me u osam. (one call)

items.en: to be sending / to send (off)
items.note: Yesterday's šaljem class + the prefix po- = the perfective.

items.hr: javljati se / javiti se — javljam se / javim se
items.en: to keep in touch / to get in touch (once)
items.note: Both take the DATIVE: javiti se komu. Also: to answer the phone.

items.en: to be answering / to answer (once)
items.note: odgovoriti na poruku = to answer a message (na + A).

items.en: to be writing / to write (and finish)
to be getting / to get, receive
items.note: Dobivam puno mailova. (constantly) vs Dobio sam tvoj mail. (that one)

items.en: I called you three times!
items.note: Repeated attempts → imperfective zvati.

items.en: I'll call you tonight.
items.note: One future call → perfective nazvati.

items.en: Get in touch with me when you arrive!
items.note: Perfective imperative for one concrete check-in. Clitics: mi (dat) + se.

items.hr: Ona mi se javlja svaki tjedan.
items.en: She gets in touch with me every week.
items.note: Habit → imperfective javljati se. Clitic order: mi → se.

items.en: I answered your message.
items.note: One completed answer → odgovoriti.

items.hr: Nazovi me sutra! / Zvao me je cijeli dan.
items.en: Call me tomorrow! / He was calling me all day.
items.note: The day's flagship pair: one call → nazovi; all-day process → zvao.

97.title: Yesterday's communications — narration with clitics
97.intro: Now narrate a whole day of calls and messages in the perfekt, stacking the clitics as you go: aux → dative → accusative. Every line here is a template for describing YOUR yesterday.

items.en: Yesterday mom called me.
items.note: nazvala — one completed call, feminine participle.

items.en: I sent it (the message) to you yesterday.
items.note: The chain: sam (aux) → ti (dative) → je (accusative, = poruku). This is the plan's showcase sentence.

items.en: I was writing emails all morning.
items.note: Process, duration → imperfective pisati.

items.en: I wrote only two messages.
items.note: Completed count → perfective napisati.

items.hr: Javio sam se šefu prije podne.
items.en: I got in touch with the boss before noon.
items.note: javiti se + dative: šefu.

items.en: You didn't answer my message!
items.note: nisi is a stressed word, so the clitic mi tucks in right after it.

items.en: I answered you right away!
items.note: Her reply — feminine odgovorila.

items.hr: Dobio sam tvoju razglednicu iz Splita.
items.en: I got your postcard from Split.

items.hr: Zvala sam te, ali nisi se javio.
items.en: I called you, but you didn't pick up.
items.note: javiti se = also 'to answer the phone'. Negative: nisi se javio.

items.hr: Nazvat ću je sutra i sve ću joj objasniti.
items.en: I'll call her tomorrow and explain everything to her.
items.note: je = her (accusative, after nazvati); joj = to her (dative, after objasniti).

97.title: Right aspect, right clitic
97.intro: Pick the right member of each pair, then stack the clitics. The test of today: 'Call me tomorrow!' and 'He was calling me all day' must come out with different verbs.

questions.prompt: Which verb means 'to call' as ONE completed call?
questions.explanation: nazvati is the perfective: one completed call. zvati is the ongoing/repeated calling.

questions.prompt: ___ me sutra! (Call me tomorrow!)
questions.explanation: One concrete future call → perfective imperative: Nazovi me sutra! (Zovi me would suggest 'keep calling me'.)

questions.prompt: 'He was calling me all day.' = ?
questions.explanation: All-day process → imperfective zvati → zvao. Nazvao (perfective) can't stretch over a whole day of ringing.

questions.prompt: Complete with two clitics: 'Javi ___ ___ kad stigneš!' (get in touch with ME)
questions.explanation: javiti se + dative: Javi mi se! The chain: dative (mi) before se.

questions.prompt: Give the ja-form of poslati (perfective 'send').
questions.explanation: poslati conjugates like slati: šaljem → pošaljem. Poslat ću ti = futur; pošaljem = e.g. 'čim pošaljem' (as soon as I send).

questions.prompt: Which aspect fits? 'Cijelo jutro sam ___ mailove.'
questions.explanation: cijelo jutro = duration → imperfective pisati → pisao. Napisao sam would need a completed result, not a stretch of time.

questions.prompt: Poslao sam ___ jučer. (it — the message — to you)
questions.explanation: Dative before accusative: Poslao sam ti je. (sam → ti → je). 'je ti' reverses the chain and is impossible.

questions.prompt: Build the sentence: 'I called you, but you didn't pick up.' (female caller)
questions.answer: Zvala sam te, ali nisi se javio.
questions.explanation: First clause: sam → te after the participle. Second clause: nisi is stressed, so se follows it — nisi se javio.

questions.prompt: Translate: 'I answered her immediately.' (male speaker, odgovoriti)
questions.explanation: Odgovorio sam joj odmah. — one completed answer → perfective; joj = dative 'to her'.

98.title: Direction phrases — the navigation toolkit
98.intro: Directions chain vi-imperatives with landmarks in the right case: do + G (up to), pokraj + G (past), preko puta + G (opposite), u + A (into a street). Learn these twelve and you can both ask for and give any route in a Croatian city.

items.hr: Oprostite, kako mogu doći do kolodvora?
items.en: Excuse me, how can I get to the station?
items.note: The question that starts it all: doći do + genitive.

items.en: Turn left. / Turn right.
items.note: skrenuti → skrenem → imperative skreni/skrenite (the krenuti family).

items.en: Go straight ahead.
Go straight to the traffic lights.
items.note: do + genitive: do semafora, do trga, do mosta.

items.en: At the crossroads turn right.
items.note: na križanju — locative for where you are when you turn.

items.en: across the street from the bank
items.note: preko puta + genitive = opposite. Kafić je preko puta banke.

items.en: around the corner
items.note: Pekara je odmah iza ugla. — iza + genitive.

items.hr: prva ulica lijevo, druga ulica desno
items.en: first street on the left, second street on the right
Cross the street.
items.note: prijeći → prijeđem → imperative prijeđi/prijeđite.

items.en: Go past the post office.
items.note: pokraj + genitive: pokraj pošte, pokraj parka.

items.en: on the other side of the street

items.hr: Je li daleko? — Ne, pet minuta pješice.
items.en: Is it far? — No, five minutes on foot.
items.note: Yesterday's pješice pays off immediately.

98.title: A route, step by step: from home to the shop
98.intro: A complete route — the exact monologue to rebuild afterwards about your own street. Each step is one imperative + one landmark; say them in order, then close your eyes and repeat the chain.

items.hr: Izađite iz zgrade i skrenite lijevo.
items.en: Exit the building and turn left.
items.note: Step 1: iz + genitive (out of the building).

items.en: Go straight to the traffic lights.
items.note: Step 2.

items.en: At the lights, cross the street.
items.note: Step 3: na semaforu = locative.

items.en: Go past the pharmacy and the bakery.
items.note: Step 4: pokraj + genitive, twice — ljekarne, pekare.

items.en: Take the first street on the right.
items.note: Turning INTO a street = u + accusative: u prvu ulicu.

items.hr: Trgovina je odmah iza ugla, preko puta parka.
items.en: The shop is just around the corner, opposite the park.
items.note: Two location phrases stacked: iza ugla + preko puta parka.

items.en: All together: five minutes on foot.

items.hr: Ako vidite crkvu, otišli ste predaleko!
items.en: If you see the church, you've gone too far!
items.note: The sentence every local ends directions with. predaleko = too far.

98.title: Navigate or get lost
98.intro: Imperatives, the right case after each preposition, and finally following a multi-step route. If you can pass this, you can find any pekara in Croatia.

questions.prompt: 'Turn right' (polite/plural) is:
questions.explanation: Vi-imperative of skrenuti: skrenite. The infinitive or present form is not a command.

questions.prompt: Idite ravno ___ semafora.
questions.explanation: do + genitive = up to: do semafora. It marks the endpoint of the straight stretch.

questions.prompt: Kafić je preko puta ___ . (opposite the bank)
questions.explanation: preko puta + genitive: preko puta banke. banku is the accusative, banci the dative/locative.

questions.prompt: Complete the command: '___ ulicu!' (Cross — vi-form of prijeći)
questions.explanation: prijeći → prijeđem → Prijeđite ulicu! Watch the đ and the ije.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Skrenite u ___ ulicu desno.' (first)
questions.explanation: Turning into a street = u + accusative, and prva agrees with ulicu: u prvu ulicu.

questions.prompt: Translate: 'The bakery is just around the corner.'
questions.explanation: iza + genitive: iza ugla. odmah (right/just) makes it sound native.

questions.prompt: Chain two steps: 'At the lights cross the street and go past the post office.'
questions.answer: Na semaforu prijeđite ulicu i prođite pokraj pošte.
questions.explanation: na semaforu (locative — where), prijeđite + accusative (ulicu), prođite pokraj + genitive (pošte).

questions.prompt: Follow the route: 'Prođite pokraj pošte, prijeđite ulicu i trgovina je preko puta parka.' What do you do right after the post office?
questions.explanation: The chain is: past the post office → prijeđite ulicu (cross the street) → the shop is opposite the park.

99.title: The clitic chain under pressure
99.intro: The template never changes: li → auxiliary → DATIVE → ACCUSATIVE/GENITIVE → se — and the auxiliary je always goes dead last. Today you drill it at question speed: 'Možeš li mi ga poslati?' has to come out as one breath, not four decisions.

items.hr: Možeš li mi ga poslati? — Mogu, poslat ću ti ga odmah.
items.en: Can you send it to me? — I can, I'll send it to you right away.
items.note: Question: li → mi (dat) → ga (acc). Answer: ću → ti → ga. Same chain, new clitics.

items.hr: Jesi li mu je dao? — Jesam, dao sam mu je jučer.
items.en: Did you give it (f.) to him? — I did, I gave it to him yesterday.
items.note: li → mu (dat) → je (acc). je here = 'it/her', not the auxiliary.

items.hr: Hoćeš li nam ih pokazati? — Pokazat ću vam ih večeras.
items.en: Will you show them to us? — I'll show them to you tonight.
items.note: nam/vam (dat) before ih (acc) — always.

items.hr: Možete li mi je opisati? — Mogu, opisat ću vam je.
items.en: Can you describe her to me? — I can, I'll describe her to you.

items.hr: Sjećaš li ga se? — Sjećam ga se jako dobro.
items.en: Do you remember him? — I remember him very well.
items.note: sjećati se takes the GENITIVE: ga sits in the ACC/GEN slot, se after it.

items.en: She gave it to me.
items.note: The auxiliary je comes after everything: mi → ga → je. Memorize this sentence as the template.

items.en: He laughed at him.
items.note: With se, the auxiliary je disappears entirely: not 'se je'.

items.hr: Kupit ću joj ga za rođendan.
items.en: I'll buy it for her for her birthday.
items.note: ću → joj (dat) → ga (acc).

items.en: Give them to me!
items.note: Imperatives host the clitics immediately after: daj → mi → ih.

items.en: Why didn't you send it to me?
items.note: After zašto, the clitics mi ga grab second position — before the stressed nisi.

items.en: I'll introduce her to you tomorrow.
items.note: ću → ti → je: aux, dative, accusative — the chain one more time.

items.hr: Vidjet ćemo se sutra. — Javit ću ti se kad krenem.
items.en: See you tomorrow. — I'll get in touch when I set off.
items.note: se closes the chain: ćemo se; ću ti se.

99.title: Clitic order gauntlet
99.intro: Nine rounds, mostly under time pressure in your head: build the question, fire back the answer, never let a clitic jump the queue.

questions.prompt: The clitic chain, in order, is:
options: li → aux → dative → accusative → se → je
li → dative → aux → accusative → je → se
aux → li → accusative → dative → se → je
je → aux → dative → accusative → se → li
questions.answer: li → aux → dative → accusative → se → je
questions.explanation: li first, then the auxiliaries (sam, ću, bih…), then dative, then accusative/genitive, then se — and the auxiliary je last of all.

questions.prompt: 'Can you send it to me?' = ?
questions.explanation: li right after the verb, then dative (mi), then accusative (ga): Možeš li mi ga poslati?

questions.prompt: 'She gave it to me.' = ?
questions.explanation: mi (dat) → ga (acc) → je (aux, always last). And je can never stand first.

questions.prompt: Complete with two clitics: 'Poslat ću ___ ___ sutra.' (to you + it, masculine)
questions.explanation: ću (aux) → ti (dative) → ga (accusative): Poslat ću ti ga sutra.

questions.prompt: Build the question: 'Did you give it (f.) to him?'
questions.explanation: Jesi hosts the chain: li → mu (dat) → je (acc). Here je is 'it/her', sitting in the accusative slot.

questions.prompt: Build the question: 'Will you show them to us?'
questions.explanation: li → nam (dative) → ih (accusative), then the infinitive. The chain survives any verb you hang it on.

questions.prompt: Answer at speed: 'Možeš li mi je poslati?' — 'Mogu, poslat ću ___ ___ odmah.'
questions.explanation: Flip the persons: mi → ti, keep je (it, f.): Poslat ću ti je odmah.

questions.prompt: Build the reproach: 'Why didn't you send it to me?'
questions.explanation: zašto takes first position, so the clitics mi ga claim second — BEFORE the stressed negative nisi.

questions.prompt: On ___ nasmijao. (He laughed at him.)
questions.explanation: Dative (mu) before se — and with se the auxiliary je vanishes: On mu se nasmijao, never 'se je'.

100.title: The ići family: one base, four precise perfectives
100.intro: Day 100! The perfect emblem of how far you've come: the imperfective base ići (go) plus a fleet of perfectives that pin down the exact moment — krenuti (set off), otići (leave), doći (arrive/come), stići (make it). Learn the present stems and participles, then narrate the trip below.

items.en: to go (be going) → I go; went
items.note: The imperfective base: the journey itself, the process.

items.en: to set off → I set off; set off
items.note: The -nuti class: krenem, kreneš… krenu.

items.en: to leave, go away → I leave; left
items.note: Present odem, but the participle keeps the t: otišao.

items.en: to come, arrive → I come; came
items.note: The đ family: dođem, dođeš… dođu.

items.en: to make it, arrive (in time) → I arrive; arrived
items.note: stignem — the -nem present; stigao/stigla in the perfekt.

items.hr: Krenuo sam od kuće u osam.
items.en: I set off from home at eight.
items.note: The trip starts: krenuti + od + genitive.

items.en: I went by tram to the station.
items.note: The journey in progress → imperfective ići + instrumental.

items.en: The train left on time.
items.note: na vrijeme = on time — rarer than kašnjenje, treasure it.

items.hr: Stigao sam u Zadar točno u podne.
items.en: I arrived in Zadar exactly at noon.
items.note: stići = you made it; u + accusative for the destination.

items.en: I left work early.
items.note: otići s + genitive: s posla.

items.hr: Doći ću po tebe u pet.
items.en: I'll come pick you up at five.
items.note: doći po + accusative = come to fetch someone.

items.hr: Dok sam išao kući, nazvala me žena.
items.en: While I was walking home, my wife called me.
items.note: Imperfective in progress (išao), interrupted by a perfective event (nazvala) — the aspect system in one sentence.

100.title: Motion-verb precision test
100.intro: Conjugations first, then choosing the exact family member. If you can narrate a door-to-door trip without reaching for 'ići' five times, you've earned your day-100 badge.

questions.prompt: doći — the ja-form is:
questions.explanation: doći → dođem, dođeš, dođe… dođu. The đ appears in every present form.

questions.prompt: krenuti — the ja-form is:
questions.explanation: -nuti → -nem: krenem, kreneš… krenu. (krećem belongs to kretati, the imperfective cousin.)

questions.prompt: The masculine past participle of stići is:
questions.explanation: stići → stigao, stigla, stiglo. Stigao sam! = I made it!

questions.prompt: ___ sam od kuće u sedam. (set off)
questions.explanation: The start of a trip → krenuti: Krenuo sam od kuće u sedam. išao = was going, došao = came, stigao = arrived.

questions.prompt: Complete: '___ sam u Split u podne.' (arrived / made it — stići, male speaker)
questions.explanation: Stigao sam u Split u podne. — stići for the arrival moment, u + accusative for the destination.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Ana je ___ s posla ranije.' (left — otići)
questions.explanation: otići → feminine participle otišla: Ana je otišla s posla ranije.

questions.prompt: Build the sentence: 'While I was walking home, my wife called me.'
questions.answer: Dok sam išao kući, nazvala me žena.
questions.explanation: Imperfective backdrop (dok sam išao) + perfective event (nazvala me). kući with no preposition = homeward.

questions.prompt: Translate: 'I'll come pick you up at five.' (doći po…)
questions.answer: Doći ću po tebe u pet.
accepted: Doći ću po tebe u pet
doći ću po tebe u pet.
questions.explanation: Doći ću po tebe u pet. — doći po + accusative (the stressed tebe after a preposition), -ći verbs keep the full infinitive before ću.

100.title: Day 100: door to door — and a toast
100.intro: One hundred days. The exam: narrate yesterday's trip using the whole ići family, with your favourite examiner listening. Then celebrate — you've earned every line of this.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Znaš li koji je danas dan? Stoti dan mog hrvatskog!
lines.en: Do you know what day it is? Day one hundred of my Croatian!

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Stoti?! Onda zaslužuješ svečani ispit. Ispričaj mi jučerašnje putovanje, od vrata do vrata.
lines.en: One hundred?! Then you deserve a ceremonial exam. Tell me about yesterday's trip, door to door.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Može. Krenuo sam od kuće u osam i išao sam tramvajem do glavnog kolodvora.
lines.en: Sure. I set off from home at eight and went by tram to the main station.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Dobro. I onda?
lines.en: Good. And then?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Vlak je krenuo na vrijeme, ali je stigao u Zadar s malim kašnjenjem.
lines.en: The train left on time, but arrived in Zadar with a small delay.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: S malim kašnjenjem — kao pravi hrvatski vlak. Nastavi!
lines.en: With a small delay — like a true Croatian train. Continue!

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Došao sam u hotel oko dva, a poslije sam otišao na plažu.
lines.en: I got to the hotel around two, and afterwards I went off to the beach.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Savršeno! Krenuo, išao, stigao, došao, otišao — cijela obitelj glagola!
lines.en: Perfect! Set off, went, arrived, came, left — the whole verb family!

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Prije sto dana znao sam reći samo 'dobar dan' i 'pivo, molim'.
lines.en: A hundred days ago I only knew how to say 'good day' and 'a beer, please'.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: A sada mi pričaš o redu vožnje i kašnjenjima. Ponosna sam na tebe.
lines.en: And now you're telling me about timetables and delays. I'm proud of you.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Kad bih morao birati ponovno, opet bih učio hrvatski — zbog tebe.
lines.en: If I had to choose again, I would learn Croatian again — because of you.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ajme, sad ćeš me rasplakati! Idemo proslaviti — častim večerom.
lines.en: Oh no, you're going to make me cry! Let's go celebrate — dinner's on me.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Može! A sljedećih sto dana? Idemo dalje!
lines.en: Deal! And the next hundred days? Onward!
